possible_reason: Fungal pathogen that overwinters on fallen leaves and releases spores during wet, cool spring weather.
symptoms: Olive-green to black scaly lesions on leaves and fruit, causing leaf drop and deformed apples.
precaution_method: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn. Prune trees to ensure good air circulation and rapid drying.
other_suggestion: Apply preventative fungicide sprays starting at the green tip stage before rain events.

possible_reason: Fungal pathogen infects through wounds, dead bark, or mummified fruits left in the orchard.
symptoms: Circular brown lesions on leaves, black rotting spots on fruits, and cankers on branches.
precaution_method: Remove dead wood, cankers, and infected fruits from the orchard and burn them.
other_suggestion: Sanitize pruning tools between cuts to prevent spreading infection.

possible_reason: Fungus requires both apple trees and nearby cedar/juniper trees to complete its life cycle.
symptoms: Bright orange or yellow spots on leaves with tiny black dots in the center, causing premature defoliation.
precaution_method: Remove eastern red cedar trees within a 1-to-2-mile radius of the orchard if possible.
other_suggestion: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties when establishing a new orchard.

possible_reason: Optimal growing conditions, proper nutrition, and effective pest management.
symptoms: Vibrant green leaves, strong branches, and unblemished fruit development.
precaution_method: Maintain regular watering, pruning, and fertilization schedules.
other_suggestion: Continue routine scouting for early signs of pests or seasonal diseases.

disease: Corn Cercospora Leaf Spot (Gray Leaf Spot)
possible_reason: Overwinters in corn debris on the soil surface; thrives in prolonged warm, humid weather.
symptoms: Rectangular, pale brown to gray lesions that run parallel to the leaf veins.
precaution_method: Practice crop rotation (non-host crops for 1-2 years) and employ deep tillage to bury infected debris.
other_suggestion: Select highly resistant corn hybrids for future planting.

possible_reason: Airborne spores blown in from warmer climates; favored by cool, moist conditions.
symptoms: Small, powdery, brick-red to brown pustules on both upper and lower leaf surfaces.
precaution_method: Plant early to avoid peak spore showers later in the season.
other_suggestion: Usually not economically devastating unless it hits the crop before the tasseling stage.

possible_reason: Fungus overwinters in crop residue; infections occur during moderate temperatures with heavy dew.
symptoms: Large, cigar-shaped, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves, progressing from bottom to top.
precaution_method: Use a 1-to-2-year crop rotation away from corn and manage residue via tillage.
other_suggestion: Scout lower leaves early in the season to catch the infection before it reaches the ear leaves.

possible_reason: Good soil fertility, sufficient rainfall, and absence of major pathogens.
symptoms: Dark green foliage, sturdy stalks, and well-developed ears.
precaution_method: Maintain proper weed control to prevent nutrient competition.
other_suggestion: Monitor soil moisture to prevent drought stress during the crucial tasseling and silking stages.

possible_reason: Overwinters in mummified berries and cane lesions; spreads via rain splash during warm, wet weather.
symptoms: Light brown lesions on leaves with dark borders; berries turn hard, black, and shriveled (mummies).
precaution_method: Prune out and destroy infected vines and all mummified berries left on the trellis.
other_suggestion: Keep the grapevine canopy open to allow rapid drying of leaves and better spray penetration.

possible_reason: Complex of wood-rotting fungi infecting vines primarily through large pruning wounds.
symptoms: Leaves show 'tiger-stripe' necrosis; berries develop dark purplish spots (measles). Vine may suddenly collapse.
precaution_method: Avoid pruning during wet weather. Apply wound protectant pastes immediately after pruning.
other_suggestion: Severely infected vines cannot be cured and should be uprooted and burned to protect neighboring vines.

possible_reason: Fungal spores spread during periods of heavy rainfall and high humidity late in the season.
symptoms: Dark red to brown angular spots on leaves, which may dry up and drop off, weakening the vine.
precaution_method: Remove and destroy infected fallen leaves. Improve air circulation through canopy management.
other_suggestion: Avoid overhead irrigation, which keeps foliage wet and promotes fungal growth.

possible_reason: Excellent canopy management, balanced soil nutrition, and proactive disease prevention.
symptoms: Lush green leaves, clean canes, and uniform, unblemished fruit clusters.
precaution_method: Continue regular pruning and trellis maintenance.
other_suggestion: Maintain a preventative fungicide program (like sulfur dust) early in the season to keep powdery mildew away.

possible_reason: Fungus survives in soil and crop debris; affects older, lower leaves first during alternating wet and dry weather.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-like) on older leaves, causing them to turn yellow and die.
precaution_method: Use certified disease-free seeds and practice a 3-to-4-year crop rotation.
other_suggestion: Ensure plants have adequate nitrogen; stressed plants are more susceptible to early blight.

possible_reason: Highly destructive pathogen that spreads rapidly in cool, wet, and humid conditions.
symptoms: Water-soaked, dark green to black patches on leaves, white fuzzy growth on undersides, and rotting tubers.
precaution_method: Destroy cull piles (discarded potatoes). Ensure good soil hilling to protect tubers from spores washing down.
other_suggestion: This disease caused the Irish Potato Famine. Treat immediately upon first sighting or use preventative sprays.

possible_reason: Good seed quality, well-draining soil, and favorable weather conditions.
symptoms: Vigorous green foliage and smooth, firm tubers forming underground.
precaution_method: Maintain even soil moisture to prevent tuber cracking or hollow heart.
other_suggestion: Monitor the field for pests like the Colorado Potato Beetle during the growing season.

possible_reason: Transmitted by several aphid species feeding on infected plants, or spread via infected seed canes.
symptoms: Mottling or mosaic patterns of light and dark green on young leaves; stunting of plant growth.
precaution_method: Always use healthy, certified, tissue-cultured seed sets for planting.
other_suggestion: Remove and destroy infected clumps (roguing) immediately. Viruses cannot be cured with chemicals.

possible_reason: Enters through borer holes or infected seed material; spreads quickly in waterlogged soils.
symptoms: Leaves wither from top to bottom; split canes show reddened tissue with transverse white bands and emit a sour, alcoholic smell.
precaution_method: Uproot and burn infected clumps. Rotate with non-host crops like paddy. Avoid waterlogging.

treatment.dosage_per_acre: 500 g (used primarily as a preventative seed-sett dip)

other_suggestion: Known as the 'cancer of sugarcane'. Do not use seed canes from fields with a history of red rot.

possible_reason: Spores are wind-borne; disease thrives in high humidity, warm days, and cool nights.
symptoms: Elongated yellowish spots on leaves that enlarge and turn into reddish-brown pustules erupting from the leaf surface.
precaution_method: Plant rust-resistant varieties and maintain optimal row spacing for air circulation.
other_suggestion: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which produces lush, vulnerable foliage.

possible_reason: Transmitted primarily by the sugarcane aphid (Melanaphis sacchari) and through infected stalks.
symptoms: Yellowing of the midrib on the lower surface of leaves, spreading out; severe stunting and yield loss.
precaution_method: Use virus-free, tissue-cultured seedlings. Do not practice ratoon cropping in heavily infected fields.
other_suggestion: There is no chemical cure. Focus entirely on vector control and clean planting material.

possible_reason: Excellent field sanitation, use of clean seed setts, and optimal water management.
symptoms: Tall, thick canes with uniform green foliage and strong internodes.
precaution_method: Keep field borders clear of weeds that harbor insect pests.
other_suggestion: Ensure timely application of basal and top-dressing fertilizers for maximum yield.

possible_reason: Parasitic algae thriving in poor drainage, highly acidic soils, and heavily shaded, humid areas.
symptoms: Raised, velvety, orange to rust-colored circular patches on the upper surface of mature leaves.
precaution_method: Improve soil drainage, reduce shade to allow 50% sunlight, and apply lime if soil pH is too low.
other_suggestion: Prune overcrowded bushes to increase ventilation. Algal spot indicates generally weak bush health.

possible_reason: Fungal infection occurring frequently during the wet monsoon season on soft, young growth.
symptoms: Irregular dark brown patches on leaves, often starting at the margins or tips, leading to leaf drop.
precaution_method: Maintain a regular plucking schedule. Do not let water stagnate around the roots.
other_suggestion: Pluck and destroy infected leaves early to stop the spread across the garden.

possible_reason: Favored by continuous drizzle, fog, and high humidity in cooler elevations.
symptoms: Small, circular brown spots with a light gray center and dark purple border, resembling a bird's eye.
precaution_method: Regulate shade tree canopy to ensure adequate sunlight penetration.
other_suggestion: Avoid plucking operations when bushes are heavily wet to minimize spreading spores manually.

possible_reason: Often a secondary infection following sun scorch, hail damage, or pest attacks.
symptoms: Large, irregular brown necrotic areas on older leaves, sometimes covered with tiny black fruiting bodies.
precaution_method: Protect plants from severe sun scorch through proper shade tree management.
other_suggestion: Ensure the tea bushes are well-nourished with potassium to increase natural resistance.

possible_reason: Attacks bushes weakened by nutrient deficiency, drought, or poor soil conditions.
symptoms: Reddish-brown spots on leaves that eventually turn gray in the center and crack.
precaution_method: Improve soil fertility, specifically focusing on potash (potassium) application.
other_suggestion: Implement a strong weed management program to reduce competition for soil nutrients.

possible_reason: Fungal disease occurring primarily in crowded, unpruned bushes with poor aeration.
symptoms: Tiny, distinct grayish-white spots with dark brown margins on the leaf surface.
precaution_method: Skiff or selectively prune bushes to open up the canopy and reduce crowding.
other_suggestion: Improve overall garden hygiene and ensure plucking rounds are strictly maintained.

possible_reason: Excellent garden management, balanced shade, and consistent plucking.
symptoms: Flush with bright green shoots (two leaves and a bud) ready for harvest.
precaution_method: Maintain regular plucking rounds and balanced NPK fertilizer application.
other_suggestion: Monitor the underside of leaves for red spider mites during extended dry spells.

possible_reason: Bacteria spread by splashing rain, overhead irrigation, and contaminated seeds.
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn angular; scabby, raised spots on green fruits.
precaution_method: Avoid overhead watering; use drip irrigation. Do not work in the field when foliage is wet.
other_suggestion: Rotate tomatoes and peppers with non-host crops for at least 2 years to break the cycle.

possible_reason: Fungus survives in soil debris; starts on lower foliage in warm, damp weather.
symptoms: Brown spots with concentric target-like rings on older leaves; yellowing of surrounding tissue.
precaution_method: Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground. Apply a thick layer of organic mulch.
other_suggestion: Prune and remove the lowest branches once the plant is established to improve airflow.

possible_reason: Aggressive pathogen thriving in cool, wet, and highly humid environments.
symptoms: Large, dark, water-soaked blotches on leaves and stems; firm, dark brown rotting spots on fruits.
precaution_method: Ensure excellent air circulation and destroy infected plants completely. Do not compost them.
other_suggestion: Late blight can destroy an entire crop in days. Apply preventative fungicides if cool, wet weather is forecast.

possible_reason: Thrives in extremely high humidity and poor airflow, very common in greenhouse tomatoes.
symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on the upper leaf surface with olive-green to brown mold on the underside.
precaution_method: Improve ventilation in greenhouses. Space plants adequately and remove lower foliage.
other_suggestion: Reduce relative humidity below 85% if growing indoors or in a high tunnel.

possible_reason: Highly contagious; mechanically transmitted via human hands, contaminated tools, or seeds.
symptoms: Mottled light and dark green leaves, stunted growth, and sometimes internal browning of fruits.
precaution_method: Wash hands with soap and milk before handling plants. Disinfect pruners between every plant.
other_suggestion: Uproot and burn infected plants immediately. Do not use tobacco products near tomato plants.

possible_reason: Fungus splashes from soil onto lower leaves during heavy rain or overhead watering.
symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots with dark borders and grayish-white centers on lower leaves.
precaution_method: Clear all weed hosts (like nightshade). Apply mulch immediately after planting to prevent soil splash.
other_suggestion: Pick off and dispose of infected leaves as soon as they appear to slow the spread up the plant.

possible_reason: Tiny sap-sucking pests that explode in population during hot, dry, and dusty conditions.
symptoms: Leaves show tiny yellow or white stippling dots; fine webbing is visible on the undersides or between stems.
precaution_method: Keep plants well-watered. Hose down foliage occasionally to wash off dust and dislodge mites.
other_suggestion: Mites build chemical resistance incredibly fast. Alternate miticides or use insecticidal soap/neem oil.

possible_reason: Favored by high humidity, warm temperatures, and heavy fruit loads stressing the plant.
symptoms: Small brown spots with a yellow halo on leaves; fruits develop sunken lesions with target-like rings.
precaution_method: Ensure adequate plant spacing. Prune excess suckers and foliage to increase light penetration.
other_suggestion: Maintain a balanced fertilization program so plants do not become nutrient-stressed during heavy fruiting.

possible_reason: Virus transmitted exclusively by the sweetpotato whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).
symptoms: Upward curling and severe yellowing of leaf margins; drastic stunting of plant and flower drop.
precaution_method: Use reflective UV mulches to disorient whiteflies. Grow resistant tomato varieties.
other_suggestion: Use yellow sticky traps around the field borders to monitor and trap adult whiteflies.

possible_reason: Proper staking, disease-free seed, excellent watering practices, and good pest management.
symptoms: Sturdy green stems, lush foliage, and abundant flowers/fruit setting without blemishes.
precaution_method: Maintain consistent, even moisture to prevent blossom end rot.
other_suggestion: Continue regular pruning of suckers to ensure energy is directed toward fruit production.
